text.title: Contract-First Anti-patterns
text.summary: Common failures: code before spec, hand-editing generated stubs, copy-pasted schemas, and context-window truncation.

antipattern.description: Agent modifies server code first, then back-fills the OpenAPI spec to match the implementation.
antipattern.reason: Code-first produces inconsistent APIs across endpoints; the spec becomes documentation, not a contract. Invert the flow: spec changes only until spec is committed.

antipattern.description: Hand-editing generated server stubs "just this once" instead of modifying the spec and regenerating.
antipattern.reason: Hand-edited generated code is overwritten on the next regen; the change is silently lost, causing a regression.

antipattern.description: Copy-pasting schema definitions across paths instead of using $ref to components/schemas.
antipattern.reason: Duplicated schemas diverge; changing one definition does not update the others, creating inconsistent validation.

antipattern.description: Agent regenerates stubs and overwrites hand-written implementation in service classes.
antipattern.reason: Generated code overwrites real business logic; use .openapi-generator-ignore to mark service classes as protected from regen.

antipattern.description: Loading the entire large spec in one LLM context; agent drops $ref anchors when summarizing.
antipattern.reason: $ref truncation causes agents to inline schema content, defeating the reuse architecture. Edit one path/schema file at a time.

rule.statement: Every spec PR requires human approval; automated merge of spec changes is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Breaking changes and business-logic implications cannot be validated by linters alone; human review is the final gate.
